title: Rugbrød
subtitle: Danish sourdough rye loaf with sunflower seeds, pumpkin seeds, and caraway
excerpt: A dense, sliceable loaf made from a high proportion of rye flour leavened with sourdough starter and packed with sunflower seeds, pumpkin seeds, flaxseed, and caraway. There is no shaping: the stiff, wet mixture is stirred together and pressed into a loaf tin for a long slow ferment before baking. The result cuts into thin, firm slices that hold toppings without tearing. The standard base for open-faced Danish sandwiches.
sourceNotes: Rugbrød is a central part of Danish food culture. Dense seeded rye loaves leavened with sourdough are documented in Danish home-economics texts from the early twentieth century. The high-rye, seed-packed format became standard in Danish households through mid-century publications. The characteristic dense crumb, sour flavour, and extended fermentation are consistent across Danish baking literature from that period.

content.text: Because rye ferments faster than wheat,

is the most important variable. The loaf goes into the oven when a dome sits just above the tin rim and small bubbles appear at the surface; not before, and not after the dome starts to drop. There is no shaping: the stiff, wet mixture is stirred and pressed into the tin.

Grease a 1kg loaf tin well. Mix the rye flour, strong bread flour, and salt in a large bowl. Add the water and sourdough starter and stir vigorously until fully combined; the mixture will look like a thick, sticky batter rather than a kneadable dough. Stir in all the seeds. The mixture should drop heavily from a spoon.

Press the mixture firmly into the prepared tin with wet hands, smoothing the surface flat. The tin should be about two-thirds full. Cover with a damp cloth or cling film. Leave at room temperature (18 to 22°C) for 6 to 10 hours. The correct endpoint is a dome sitting just above the tin rim with small bubbles visible on the surface. Preheat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan) before the dome develops.

Cover the tin with foil. Bake for 45 minutes, then remove the foil and bake for a further 40 to 45 minutes until the loaf is deep brown, pulling away from the sides of the tin, and the internal temperature reads 98°C. Turn out onto a rack and leave to cool completely, at least 4 hours, before slicing. Cutting too soon produces a gummy crumb. Store wrapped in paper at room temperature and use within 5 days.
